title: Generate permutations
description: Generate all permutations of a string or array

examples.explanation: Step 1: Start with empty permutation [], used = [False, False]. Step 2: At position 0, try 'A' (unused): permutation = ['A'], used = [True, False], recurse. Step 3: In recursion, at position 1, try 'A' (used) → skip, try 'B' (unused): permutation = ['A', 'B'], used = [True, True], length = 2 → add 'AB' to result, backtrack. Step 4: Backtrack to position 0, try 'B' (unused): permutation = ['B'], used = [False, True], recurse. Step 5: In recursion, at position 1, try 'A' (unused): permutation = ['B', 'A'], used = [True, True], length = 2 → add 'BA' to result, backtrack. Result: ['AB', 'BA'].

Step 1: Start with permutation = [], used = [False, False, False]. Step 2: At position 0, try 1: permutation = [1], used = [True, False, False], recurse. Step 3: At position 1, try 1 (used) → skip, try 2: permutation = [1,2], used = [True, True, False], recurse. Step 4: At position 2, try 1 (used) → skip, try 2 (used) → skip, try 3: permutation = [1,2,3], used = [True, True, True], length = 3 → add [1,2,3] to result, backtrack. Continue similarly for all branches: after backtracking, try 3 at position 1: permutation = [1,3], used = [True, False, True], recurse, try 2 at position 2: add [1,3,2]. Backtrack to position 0, try 2: permutation = [2], used = [False, True, False], recurse, generate [2,1,3] and [2,3,1]. Try 3 at position 0: permutation = [3], used = [False, False, True], recurse, generate [3,1,2] and [3,2,1]. Result has 6 permutations.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(n*n!)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations by recursively building permutations from scratch for each position, checking for duplicates. This is the most straightforward but inefficient approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Permutations Using Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to generate all permutations of a string or array using brute force recursion with duplicate checking.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to generate all permutations of a given input (string or array) using a brute force recursive approach. The intuition is to build permutations from scratch by trying every possible element at each position, while ensuring no duplicates are generated. We use recursion to explore all possible arrangements: at each recursive call, we iterate through all elements of the input, check if an element has already been used in the current permutation (to avoid duplicates), and if not, add it to the current permutation and recurse further. When the current permutation reaches the same length as the input, we add it to the result list. This approach is straightforward but inefficient because it generates permutations by brute force exploration of all possibilities, leading to O(n*n!) time complexity where n is the length of the input. The space complexity is O(n!) to store all permutations plus O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L1: This level integrates the key subroutine (duplicate checking and recursive building) from L1 into a complete solution. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (which would generate duplicate permutations), incorrect base case handling, or inefficient duplicate checking. Real code pattern: Use a recursive function that takes the current permutation and a used array/list to track used elements, iterate through input elements, check if unused, add to permutation, mark as used, recurse, then backtrack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty permutation [], used = [False, False]. Step 2: At position 0, try 'A' (unused): permutation = ['A'], used = [True, False], recurse. Step 3: In recursion, at position 1, try 'A' (used) → skip, try 'B' (unused): permutation = ['A', 'B'], used = [True, True], length = 2 → add 'AB' to result, backtrack. Step 4: Backtrack to position 0, try 'B' (unused): permutation = ['B'], used = [False, True], recurse. Step 5: In recursion, at position 1, try 'A' (unused): permutation = ['B', 'A'], used = [True, True], length = 2 → add 'BA' to result, backtrack. Result: ['AB', 'BA'].

Step 1: Start with permutation = [], used = [False, False, False]. Step 2: At position 0, try 1: permutation = [1], used = [True, False, False], recurse. Step 3: At position 1, try 1 (used) → skip, try 2: permutation = [1,2], used = [True, True, False], recurse. Step 4: At position 2, try 1 (used) → skip, try 2 (used) → skip, try 3: permutation = [1,2,3], used = [True, True, True], length = 3 → add [1,2,3] to result, backtrack. Continue similarly for all branches: after backtracking, try 3 at position 1: permutation = [1,3], used = [True, False, True], recurse, try 2 at position 2: add [1,3,2]. Backtrack to position 0, try 2: permutation = [2], used = [False, True, False], recurse, generate [2,1,3] and [2,3,1]. Try 3 at position 0: permutation = [3], used = [False, False, True], recurse, generate [3,1,2] and [3,2,1]. Result has 6 permutations.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Permutation Building with Duplicate Checking
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that builds permutations by selecting unused elements at each step.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that builds permutations incrementally. The intuition is to use depth-first search (DFS) to explore all possible arrangements: at each step, we add an unused element to the current permutation and recurse until the permutation is complete. We maintain a 'used' array to track which elements have been included in the current permutation to avoid reusing elements and prevent duplicates. The algorithm: define a recursive function that takes the current permutation and used array as parameters. Base case: if the current permutation length equals the input length, add it to the result. Recursive case: iterate through all elements of the input; for each element, if it is not used, mark it as used, add it to the permutation, recurse, then backtrack by removing it from the permutation and marking it as unused. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n!) as it generates all permutations, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and used array. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is the main recursive engine called by the full solution in L0. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack (leading to incorrect permutations), incorrect base case condition, or not copying the permutation before adding to result (if using mutable lists). Real code pattern: Use a helper function that recursively builds permutations with a used array for tracking.

examples.trace: Step 1: Base case: permutation length 0 ≠ 2, so continue. Step 2: Loop elements: try 1 (unused): mark used[0]=True, permutation=[1], recurse. Step 3: In recursion, base case: length 1 ≠ 2, loop: try 1 (used) → skip, try 2 (unused): mark used[1]=True, permutation=[1,2], recurse. Step 4: Base case: length 2 = 2 → add [1,2] to result, return. Step 5: Backtrack: unmark used[1]=False, remove 2 from permutation. Step 6: Backtrack to outer call: unmark used[0]=False, remove 1 from permutation. Step 7: Try 2 (unused): mark used[1]=True, permutation=[2], recurse, generate [2,1] similarly.

Step 1: Base case: length 0 ≠ 2. Step 2: Try 'A': used[0]=True, permutation='A', recurse. Step 3: In recursion, try 'A' (used) → skip, try 'B' (unused): used[1]=True, permutation='AB', recurse. Step 4: Base case: length 2 = 2 → add 'AB' to result, return. Step 5: Backtrack: unmark used[1], remove 'B'. Step 6: Backtrack: unmark used[0], remove 'A'. Step 7: Try 'B': used[1]=True, permutation='B', recurse, generate 'BA' similarly.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Depth-First Search (DFS) for Permutation Generation
ladders.desc: Understand and implement DFS to explore all possible arrangements of elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using Depth-First Search (DFS) to systematically explore all possible permutations. The intuition is to treat permutation generation as a tree traversal problem: each node represents a partial permutation, and edges represent adding a new element. DFS explores one branch completely before backtracking to try other branches. The algorithm: start with an empty permutation. At each step, consider all possible elements to add next (those not yet used), add one, recurse to build the rest, then backtrack to try other choices. This ensures all permutations are generated. Time complexity is O(n!) as there are n! leaves in the tree, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth. Connection to L1: This DFS technique is the underlying method used in the recursive sub-routine in L1. Common mistakes include not exploring all branches (e.g., due to incorrect loop bounds), not handling backtracking properly, or confusion between DFS and BFS. Real code pattern: Use recursion with a loop to iterate through possible choices at each step.

examples.output: Tree: root ([]), children: [1] and [2]; [1] has child [1,2]; [2] has child [2,1]
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root []. Step 2: DFS branch 1: go to [1], then to [1,2] (leaf), add to result, backtrack to [1], then to root. Step 3: DFS branch 2: go to [2], then to [2,1] (leaf), add to result, backtrack to [2], then to root. DFS order: explore [1] branch fully first, then [2] branch.

examples.output: Tree: root (''), children: 'A' and 'B'; 'A' has child 'AB'; 'B' has child 'BA'
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root ''. Step 2: DFS branch 1: go to 'A', then to 'AB' (leaf), add to result, backtrack to 'A', then to root. Step 3: DFS branch 2: go to 'B', then to 'BA' (leaf), add to result, backtrack to 'B', then to root. DFS ensures all paths are explored.

testCases.expected: Might generate duplicate or incomplete permutations

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Used Elements with a Boolean Array
ladders.desc: Implement a mechanism to track which elements have been used in the current permutation to avoid duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using a boolean array (or list) to keep track of which elements from the input have been included in the current permutation. The intuition is that to generate permutations without repetition, we need to ensure each element is used exactly once per permutation. A boolean array 'used' of the same length as the input is maintained, where used[i] is True if the i-th element is already in the current permutation, and False otherwise. At each step, when we consider adding an element, we check if it is unused (used[i] == False), then mark it as used, add it to the permutation, and after recursion, mark it as unused again (backtracking). This ensures that in each recursive branch, elements are not reused. Time complexity for checking and updating is O(1) per element, and space complexity is O(n) for the array. Connection to L2: This tracking mechanism is essential in the DFS logic from L2 to prevent reusing elements. Common mistakes include not resetting the used array correctly during backtracking, using the wrong index, or not initializing the array properly. Real code pattern: Initialize a list of False with length n, update used[i] when adding/removing elements.

examples.input: Input: [1,2,3], used = [False, False, False], add element at index 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with used = [F, F, F]. Step 2: Check used[1] is False, so we can add element 2. Step 3: Mark used[1] = True. Step 4: After recursion, backtrack by setting used[1] = False.

examples.output: used changes: [True, False] → [True, True] → backtrack to [True, False] → [False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial used = [F, F]. Step 2: Add 'A' (index 0): used[0] = True, used = [T, F]. Step 3: Add 'B' (index 1): used[1] = True, used = [T, T]. Step 4: After adding permutation, backtrack: unmark 'B': used[1] = False, used = [T, F]. Step 5: Backtrack further: unmark 'A': used[0] = False, used = [F, F].

testCases.input: used = [True, False], add element at index 1
used = [True, True], try to add element at index 0
used = [False, False, False], add elements at indices 0,2
used = [True, False, True], backtrack element at index 2
used = [True, False, False], check if element at index 0 can be added
used = [False, True, False], add element at index 2
used = [True, False], reset all to False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array Elements with Index
ladders.desc: Perform iteration over an array or string to access each element and its index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves looping through all elements of an input array or string, typically using an index-based loop. The intuition is that to build permutations, we need to consider each element as a candidate for the next position in the permutation. We iterate from the first element to the last, accessing each element and its index. For arrays/lists, we can use a for-loop with range(len(input)) to get indices, and for strings, we can do similarly since strings are indexable in most languages. This allows us to check if an element is used (via its index) and add it to the permutation. Time complexity is O(n) per iteration, and space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. Connection to L3: This iteration is used in L3 to check and update the used array for each element. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, confusing indices with values, or not handling empty inputs. Real code pattern: Use 'for i in range(len(arr)):' to iterate, then access arr[i] for the element.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, element=1. Step 2: i=1, element=2. Step 3: i=2, element=3. Loop ends after i=2.
Step 1: i=0, char='A'. Step 2: i=1, char='B'. Loop ends.

testCases.expected: No iteration, loop does not execute

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Permutations as Arrangements
ladders.desc: Learn what a permutation is: an ordered arrangement of all elements of a set.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces permutations. A permutation is an ordered arrangement of all the elements of a set. For example, for the set {A, B}, the permutations are AB and BA. The intuition is that order matters: AB is different from BA. The number of permutations of n distinct elements is n! (n factorial), which grows rapidly. For sets with duplicates, permutations may repeat, and we need to avoid generating duplicates. This concept is fundamental because generating permutations involves creating all such arrangements. Connection to L4: Understanding permutations motivates why we iterate through elements in L4 to build these arrangements. Common mistakes include confusing permutations with combinations (where order doesn't matter), or not understanding factorial growth. Real code pattern: No code yet, just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: List all elements: 1 and 2. Step 2: Arrange them in order: put 1 first, then 2 gives [1,2]. Step 3: Put 2 first, then 1 gives [2,1]. Total: 2! = 2 permutations.

examples.output: Permutations: ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA
examples.trace: Step 1: Elements: A, B, C. Step 2: Fix A first: arrangements of B,C are BC and CB, so ABC and ACB. Step 3: Fix B first: arrangements of A,C are AC and CA, so BAC and BCA. Step 4: Fix C first: arrangements of A,B are AB and BA, so CAB and CBA. Total: 3! = 6 permutations.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Swapping O(n*n!)
approaches.summary: Use backtracking by swapping elements at different positions to generate permutations in-place. This avoids creating new arrays for each permutation until needed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Permutations Using Backtracking with Swapping
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to generate all permutations of an array/string using in-place swapping and backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution generates all permutations of an input array or string using backtracking with in-place swapping. The intuition is to treat the permutation generation as a depth-first search through a decision tree where at each level, we decide which element should occupy the current position. By swapping elements, we can generate permutations without creating new arrays for each permutation until we need to store the result. The algorithm works by fixing elements at the current index one by one, recursively generating permutations for the remaining positions, and then backtracking by swapping back to restore the original order. This approach is efficient in space because it uses O(n) recursion depth and avoids O(n!) memory for storing intermediate arrays. The time complexity is O(n*n!) because there are n! permutations and each takes O(n) time to generate. The key insight is that swapping allows us to explore all arrangements while maintaining the original array structure through backtracking. Common mistakes include forgetting to swap back after recursion (which breaks backtracking), not handling base cases correctly, and incorrectly managing indices. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete backtracking function that calls the swap-based permutation generation recursively.

examples.trace: Start with arr=[1,2,3], index=0. Call backtrack(0): For i=0 to 2: i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change, arr=[1,2,3]. Call backtrack(1): For i=1 to 2: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change, arr=[1,2,3]. Call backtrack(2): index=2 equals length, add copy [1,2,3] to result. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. i=2: swap arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[1,3,2]. Call backtrack(2): index=2 equals length, add copy [1,3,2] to result. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[1,2,3]. Backtrack from backtrack(1): swap back arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. i=1: swap arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=[2,1,3]. Call backtrack(1): For i=1 to 2: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change, arr=[2,1,3]. Call backtrack(2): add [2,1,3]. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. i=2: swap arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[2,3,1]. Call backtrack(2): add [2,3,1]. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[2,1,3]. Backtrack from backtrack(1): swap back arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=[1,2,3]. i=2: swap arr[0] and arr[2] → arr=[3,2,1]. Call backtrack(1): For i=1 to 2: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change, arr=[3,2,1]. Call backtrack(2): add [3,2,1]. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. i=2: swap arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[3,1,2]. Call backtrack(2): add [3,1,2]. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=[3,2,1]. Backtrack from backtrack(1): swap back arr[0] and arr[2] → arr=[1,2,3]. End. Result has 6 permutations.

Convert string to list ['A','B']. Start with arr=['A','B'], index=0. Call backtrack(0): For i=0 to 1: i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change, arr=['A','B']. Call backtrack(1): index=1 equals length, add copy ['A','B'] as string 'AB'. Backtrack: swap back arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. i=1: swap arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=['B','A']. Call backtrack(1): index=1 equals length, add copy ['B','A'] as string 'BA'. Backtrack: swap back arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=['A','B']. End. Result has 2 permutations.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function with Swapping
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive backtracking function that generates permutations by swapping elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that implements backtracking with swapping to generate permutations. The intuition is to recursively fix elements at each position: starting from a given index, we swap the current element with each element from the current index to the end, recursively generate permutations for the next index, and then swap back to backtrack. This ensures we explore all possible arrangements. The algorithm: function backtrack(arr, index, result): if index equals length of arr, add a copy of arr to result (base case). Otherwise, for i from index to length-1: swap arr[index] and arr[i], call backtrack(arr, index+1, result), swap back arr[index] and arr[i]. Time complexity for this function is O(n*n!) in the full context, but as a standalone, it's called recursively to generate permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the main function called to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not swapping back (which prevents exploring other permutations), modifying the array in place without copying for results (leading to incorrect outputs), and off-by-one errors in loop bounds. The function relies on swapping from L2 and recursion from L3.

examples.trace: Call backtrack(['A','B','C'],0,[]): index=0 not equal to 3. Loop i=0 to 2: i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. Call backtrack(['A','B','C'],1,[]): index=1 not equal to 3. Loop i=1 to 2: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. Call backtrack(['A','B','C'],2,[]): index=2 not equal to 3. Loop i=2 to 2: i=2: swap arr[2] and arr[2] → no change. Call backtrack(['A','B','C'],3,[]): index=3 equals 3, add copy ['A','B','C'] to result. Backtrack: swap back arr[2] and arr[2] → no change. End loop at i=2. Backtrack to index=2: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. i=2: swap arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=['A','C','B']. Call backtrack(['A','C','B'],2,result): index=2 not equal to 3. Loop i=2 to 2: i=2: swap arr[2] and arr[2] → no change. Call backtrack(['A','C','B'],3,result): add ['A','C','B']. Backtrack: swap back arr[2] and arr[2] → no change. End loop. Backtrack to index=2: swap back arr[1] and arr[2] → arr=['A','B','C']. End loop at i=2. Backtrack to index=1: swap back arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. i=1: swap arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=['B','A','C']. Recursively generate permutations similarly, adding ['B','A','C'], ['B','C','A']. Swap back. i=2: swap arr[0] and arr[2] → arr=['C','B','A']. Recursively generate, adding ['C','B','A'], ['C','A','B']. Swap back. End. Result has 6 permutations.

Call backtrack([1,2],0,[]): index=0 not equal to 2. Loop i=0 to 1: i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. Call backtrack([1,2],1,[]): index=1 not equal to 2. Loop i=1 to 1: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. Call backtrack([1,2],2,[]): index=2 equals 2, add copy [1,2] to result. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. End loop. Backtrack to index=1: swap back arr[0] and arr[0] → no change. i=1: swap arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=[2,1]. Call backtrack([2,1],1,result): index=1 not equal to 2. Loop i=1 to 1: i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. Call backtrack([2,1],2,result): add [2,1]. Backtrack: swap back arr[1] and arr[1] → no change. End loop. Backtrack to index=1: swap back arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=[1,2]. End loop. Result has 2 permutations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Swapping Elements to Generate Permutations
ladders.desc: Understand how swapping elements in-place generates different permutations.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using element swapping to generate permutations without creating new arrays. The intuition is that by swapping two elements in an array, we change their positions, creating a new arrangement. To explore all permutations, we systematically swap each element with every other element in a controlled manner, typically using recursion. For example, to permute an array of size n, we fix the first element by swapping it with each position (including itself), then recursively permute the remaining n-1 elements. This works because swapping is reversible; after exploring one branch, we swap back to restore the original order, allowing us to explore other branches (backtracking). The key insight is that swapping reduces the problem size recursively: after fixing an element at position i, the subproblem is to permute the remaining elements from i+1 to n-1. Time complexity for generating one permutation via swapping is O(n), but overall it's O(n*n!) due to n! permutations. Space is O(1) per swap, but O(n) for recursion. This connects to L1 as the mechanism used within the backtracking function. Common mistakes include swapping non-adjacent elements incorrectly, not handling duplicate swaps (which can lead to repeated permutations if not managed), and misunderstanding that swapping modifies the array in place. This logic builds on array indexing from L4 and uses recursion from L3.

examples.trace: Start with arr=[1,2,3]. Swap arr[0] and arr[1]: temp = arr[0] = 1, arr[0] = arr[1] = 2, arr[1] = temp = 1. Result: arr=[2,1,3].

examples.input: arr=['A','B','C'], recursively swap to generate all permutations
examples.trace:
Start arr=['A','B','C'].
start=0, i=0: Swap(0,0) -> ['A','B','C']. Fixed 'A'. Permuting suffix ['B','C'] yields ['A','B','C'] and ['A','C','B']. Swap back.
start=0, i=1: Swap(0,1) -> ['B','A','C']. Fixed 'B'. Permuting suffix ['A','C'] yields ['B','A','C'] and ['B','C','A']. Swap back.
start=0, i=2: Swap(0,2) -> ['C','B','A']. Fixed 'C'. Permuting suffix ['B','A'] yields ['C','B','A'] and ['C','A','B']. Swap back.

testCases.input: arr=[10,20,30], swap 0 and 2 then swap 1 and 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion for Problem Reduction
ladders.desc: Use recursion to break down permutation generation into smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. For permutations, the intuition is that generating all permutations of n elements can be reduced to fixing one element and recursively generating permutations of the remaining n-1 elements. This creates a tree of recursive calls: at each level, we choose an element for the current position and delegate the rest to recursion. The base case is when no elements are left to permute (i.e., when the current index equals the array length), at which point we have a complete permutation. This approach simplifies the problem because handling n-1 elements is easier than n, and recursion naturally explores all possibilities through repeated calls. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls, which is n! for permutations, each taking O(n) time, leading to O(n*n!). Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion call stack. This connects to L2 as recursion provides the framework in which swapping operates. Common mistakes include missing base cases (causing infinite recursion), not reducing problem size correctly (leading to incorrect permutations), and stack overflow for large n. Recursion relies on understanding function calls from L5 and uses array manipulation from L4.

examples.trace: Define function permute(arr, index): if index=2, return [arr]. Else, result=[]. For i in range(index,2): swap arr[index] and arr[i], rec_result = permute(arr, index+1), append rec_result to result, swap back. Start: permute([1,2],0): index=0<2, loop i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change, call permute([1,2],1): index=1<2, loop i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change, call permute([1,2],2): index=2=2, return [[1,2]]. Backtrack: swap back no change, append [[1,2]]. i=1: swap arr[0] and arr[1] → arr=[2,1], call permute([2,1],1): index=1<2, loop i=1: swap arr[1] and arr[1] → no change, call permute([2,1],2): return [[2,1]]. Backtrack: swap back no change, append [[2,1]]. Swap back arr to [1,2]. End with result [[1,2],[2,1]].
permute([1],0): index=0<1, loop i=0: swap arr[0] and arr[0] → no change, call permute([1],1): index=1=1, return [[1]]. Append [[1]]. Swap back no change. Result [[1]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Element Swapping
ladders.desc: Perform basic array operations: access elements by index and swap two elements.
ladders.explanation: This level covers fundamental array operations: indexing and swapping. Indexing allows access to an element at a specific position in an array, using a zero-based index. Swapping exchanges the values at two indices, which is done using a temporary variable to hold one value during the exchange. The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections, and by changing the order of elements through swapping, we can rearrange them. This is crucial for permutation generation because each permutation is a different order of the same elements. Swapping is a constant-time operation, O(1), as it involves a few assignments. In the context of permutations, swapping is used repeatedly in loops to generate different arrangements. Common mistakes include using incorrect indices (e.g., out-of-bounds), forgetting the temporary variable (which can overwrite values), and not understanding that swapping modifies the array in place. This operation connects to L3 as it is used within recursive functions to manipulate arrays. It builds on basic programming concepts from L5.

examples.trace: Start arr=[10,20,30]. Step 1: temp = arr[0] = 10. Step 2: arr[0] = arr[2] = 30. Step 3: arr[2] = temp = 10. Result: arr=[30,20,10].

examples.input: arr=['a','b','c'], access index 1 and swap with index 0
examples.trace: Start arr=['a','b','c']. Access arr[1] gives 'b'. Swap arr[0] and arr[1]: temp = arr[0] = 'a', arr[0] = arr[1] = 'b', arr[1] = temp = 'a'. Result: arr=['b','a','c'].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Function Calls
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections and how to define and call functions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces arrays and function calls. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a specific order, accessible by indices starting from 0. For example, an array [1,2,3] has elements 1 at index 0, 2 at index 1, and 3 at index 2. Functions are blocks of code that perform a specific task; they are defined with a name and parameters, and called by using the name with arguments. The intuition is that arrays allow us to group related data, and functions enable reusable logic, which is essential for implementing algorithms like permutation generation. In permutation problems, we use arrays to hold the elements to permute, and functions to encapsulate the recursive backtracking logic. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's basic. Common mistakes include confusing arrays with other data structures, using one-based indexing, and incorrect function syntax. This connects to L4 as arrays are manipulated through indexing and swapping, and functions are called recursively in higher levels.

examples.input: Define an array with elements 1, 2, 3 and access the second element
examples.trace: Create array arr = [1,2,3]. To access the second element, use arr[1] because indices start at 0: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3. So arr[1] returns 2.

examples.input: Define a function that adds two numbers and call it with 3 and 4
examples.trace: Define function add(a, b): return a + b. Call add(3, 4): a=3, b=4, return 3+4=7.

testCases.input: Function multiply(x,y): return x*y, call with 2 and 3
Function greet(name): return 'Hello ' + name, call with 'Alice'
Function square(n): return n*n, call with 5

approaches.name: Backtracking with Visited Array O(n*n!)
approaches.summary: Generate permutations by backtracking with a visited array to track used elements, building permutations incrementally. This is clearer for handling duplicates.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack and visited array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Permutations Using Backtracking with Visited Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to generate all permutations of a string or array using backtracking with a visited array.
ladders.explanation: This solution generates all permutations by building them incrementally using backtracking. We maintain a visited array to track which elements have been used in the current permutation path, ensuring each element appears exactly once. The algorithm starts with an empty permutation and recursively adds elements that haven't been visited yet. When the permutation reaches the same length as the input, it's added to the result. The visited array prevents reusing elements and handles duplicates naturally when the input contains duplicate values. The time complexity is O(n*n!) because there are n! permutations and each requires O(n) work to build. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and visited array. This approach is clearer than swapping methods for handling duplicates because the visited array explicitly tracks usage. The key insight is treating permutation generation as a depth-first search through a decision tree where each level chooses an unused element. Common mistakes include not resetting the visited array during backtracking, forgetting to copy permutations before adding to results, and inefficient handling of duplicates. The algorithm connects to L1 by implementing the complete backtracking framework that calls the recursive helper.

examples.trace:
1. Start with perm=[], visited=[False,False], result=[]
2. Level 0: Choose 'a' (index 0), perm=['a'], visited=[True,False]
3. Level 1: Only 'b' available (index 1), perm=['a','b'], visited=[True,True]
4. Perm length=2, add 'ab' to result, backtrack
5. Level 1: Reset visited[1]=False, perm=['a']
6. Level 0: Reset visited[0]=False, perm=[]
7. Level 0: Choose 'b' (index 1), perm=['b'], visited=[False,True]
8. Level 1: Only 'a' available (index 0), perm=['b','a'], visited=[True,True]
9. Perm length=2, add 'ba' to result, backtrack
10. Result=['ab','ba']

1. Start with perm=[], visited=[False,False,False], result=[]
2. First path: Choose 1→Choose 2→Choose 3, add [1,2,3]
3. Backtrack: Remove 3, choose 3→Choose 2, add [1,3,2]
4. Backtrack to root: Remove 1
5. Choose 2→Choose 1→Choose 3, add [2,1,3]
6. Backtrack: Remove 3, choose 3→Choose 1, add [2,3,1]
7. Backtrack to root: Remove 2
8. Choose 3→Choose 1→Choose 2, add [3,1,2]
9. Backtrack: Remove 2, choose 2→Choose 1, add [3,2,1]
10. All 6 permutations generated

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking Function with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that builds permutations incrementally using visited array.
ladders.explanation: This recursive function is the engine of the permutation generation. It takes the current permutation being built, a visited array tracking used elements, and the original input. The base case occurs when the current permutation length equals the input length - we add a copy to results. In the recursive case, we iterate through all elements. For each element not visited, we mark it visited, add it to the permutation, recurse, then backtrack by removing it and unmarking visited. This explores all possible orderings. The visited array ensures each element is used exactly once per permutation. For duplicates, since we check visited indices rather than values, identical elements at different indices are treated separately, but identical values at visited positions won't create duplicate permutations in the result if we're careful about ordering (though with simple visited array, duplicates may appear and need filtering). Time complexity for this function is O(n!) calls with O(n) work per call at worst. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 as the core recursive component, and to L2 as it implements the backtracking pattern. Common mistakes include modifying the original permutation list in place without copying for results, not resetting visited status during backtracking, and incorrect base case conditions.

examples.trace:
1. perm=[], visited=[F,F]
2. i=0: visited[0]=F, perm=[1], visited=[T,F], recurse
3. In recursion: perm=[1], visited=[T,F]
4. i=0: visited[0]=T, skip
5. i=1: visited[1]=F, perm=[1,2], visited=[T,T], base case: add [1,2]
6. Backtrack: perm=[1], visited=[T,F]
7. Backtrack: perm=[], visited=[F,F]
8. i=1: visited[1]=F, perm=[2], visited=[F,T], recurse
9. In recursion: perm=[2], visited=[F,T]
10. i=0: visited[0]=F, perm=[2,1], visited=[T,T], add [2,1]

1. perm="", visited=[F,F]
2. i=0: visited[0]=F, perm="a", visited=[T,F], recurse
3. In recursion: perm="a", visited=[T,F]
4. i=0: visited[0]=T, skip
5. i=1: visited[1]=F, perm="ab", visited=[T,T], base case: add "ab"
6. Backtrack: perm="a", visited=[T,F]
7. Backtrack: perm="", visited=[F,F]
8. i=1: visited[1]=F, perm="b", visited=[F,T], recurse
9. In recursion: perm="b", visited=[F,T]
10. i=0: visited[0]=F, perm="ba", visited=[T,T], add "ba"

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking Pattern for Combinatorial Generation
ladders.desc: The backtracking technique: choose, explore, unchoose.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is a systematic way to generate all combinatorial configurations by making a series of choices, exploring recursively, then undoing choices. The pattern has three phases: 1) Choose: Select an option from available choices (here, an unvisited element). 2) Explore: Recurse to make further choices. 3) Unchoose: Remove the choice to restore state for other possibilities. This ensures all paths in the decision tree are explored. For permutations, the decision tree has depth n (input length) and branching factor decreasing from n to 1. Each path from root to leaf represents one permutation. The visited array manages available choices at each level. Intuition: Imagine building permutations sequentially - at each position, pick any unused element, continue until all positions filled, then backtrack to try different picks. This is depth-first search with pruning via visited array. Time complexity arises from exploring n! leaves with O(n) work per path. Space is O(n) for recursion. Connects to L1 as the algorithmic pattern implemented, and to L3 as it uses recursion and state management. Common mistakes include forgetting the unchoose step (causing incorrect state), incorrect pruning logic, and not handling base cases properly.

testCases.input: Apply choose-explore-unchoose to generate subsets of {a,b}
Depth of decision tree for n=3
Branching factor at root for n=4
Branching factor at depth 2 for n=4
Is backtracking needed if we use copying instead of unchosing?

ladders.title: Building Block — Managing State with Visited Arrays
ladders.desc: Using a boolean array to track which elements are used in the current path.
ladders.explanation: A visited array is a boolean list where visited[i] indicates whether the i-th element of the input has been included in the current permutation being built. This prevents reusing elements within a single permutation. When we add an element to the permutation, we mark its index as visited. When we backtrack (remove the element), we unmark it. This ensures that at each recursive level, we only consider unvisited elements. For handling duplicates in the input, the visited array works on indices, so identical values at different indices are treated as distinct until combined into permutations; duplicate permutations may appear in results if not filtered, but the array correctly prevents using the same index twice. The intuition is like keeping a checklist of used items. Time complexity for visited operations is O(1) per check/mark. Space is O(n). This connects to L2 as the mechanism for tracking choices in backtracking, and to L4 as it uses array indexing. Common mistakes include forgetting to unmark during backtracking (causing elements to remain permanently visited), using wrong indices, and not initializing the array properly.

examples.trace:
Start: visited=[False, False, False]
Add index 0 (val 1): visited=[True, False, False], perm=[1]
Add index 2 (val 3): visited=[True, False, True], perm=[1, 3]
Backtrack index 2: visited=[True, False, False], perm=[1]
Add index 1 (val 2): visited=[True, True, False], perm=[1, 2]

Start: visited=[False, False, False]
Choose index 0 (val 1): visited=[True, False, False], perm=[1]
Choose index 1 (val 1): visited=[True, True, False], perm=[1, 1]
Choose index 2 (val 2): visited=[True, True, True], perm=[1, 1, 2]
Record [1, 1, 2]
Backtrack index 2: visited=[True, True, False], perm=[1, 1]

testCases.input: After visiting indices 0 and 2 of length 3
Check if index 1 is available: visited=[True,False,True]
Check if index 0 is available: visited=[True,False,True]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array Indices with Condition Checking
ladders.desc: Looping through indices and checking conditions to select elements.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating from 0 to n-1 through the input array indices, and for each index, checking a condition (whether visited[i] is false) to determine if the element can be selected. In the permutation algorithm, this loop occurs in the recursive function to try all possible next elements. The iteration ensures we consider every element as a candidate, and the condition ensures we only use unvisited elements. Intuition: At each step of building a permutation, we look at all positions in the original input and pick those that haven't been used yet. This is O(n) time per recursive call. The loop structure is typically a for-loop with range(len(input)). This connects to L3 as it's how we use the visited array, and to L5 as it builds on basic iteration. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in range, modifying the loop variable incorrectly, and not breaking out of loops properly.

examples.trace:
Loop i=0 to 2:
i=0: visited[0]=False → available
i=1: visited[1]=True → skip
i=2: visited[2]=False → available
So elements at indices 0 and 2 (10 and 30) can be chosen.

i=0: visited[0]=True → skip
i=1: visited[1]=False → available
Even though values are same, index 1 is available.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic array structure and accessing elements by index.
ladders.explanation: An array is a contiguous collection of elements accessible by numerical indices starting from 0. For an array of length n, valid indices are 0 through n-1. Each position holds an element (which can be a number, character, or any data type). Accessing arr[i] retrieves the element at index i. This is O(1) time. Arrays support iteration using loops. In the permutation problem, the input (string or list) is treated as an array, and we use indices to refer to elements. A visited array is a parallel boolean array of same length where visited[i] corresponds to input[i]. Intuition: Think of an array like a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; you can look inside any box by its number. This foundational concept is prerequisite for using visited arrays and iterating through indices. Connects to L4 as it enables the iteration and condition checking. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, and confusing indices with values.

examples.trace:
Length=3, indices 0,1,2
arr[0]=7
arr[1]=3
arr[2]=9
If we have visited=[True,False,True], then visited[0] corresponds to arr[0]=7, visited[1] to arr[1]=3, etc.

String as character array
s[0]='c'
s[1]='a'
s[2]='t'
Length=3, so last index is 2.

approaches.name: Heap's Algorithm O(n*n!)
approaches.summary: Use Heap's algorithm to generate permutations iteratively by swapping elements based on parity. This is an efficient non-recursive method.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the array and counters

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Permutations Using Heap's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete iterative implementation of Heap's algorithm to generate all permutations of an array.
ladders.explanation: Heap's algorithm is an elegant iterative method for generating all permutations of n elements. The intuition is to systematically swap elements to produce each permutation exactly once, using a parity-based approach that avoids recursion. The algorithm maintains an array of counters (one per element position) that track how many times each position has been 'processed'. At each step, it finds the rightmost element whose counter hasn't exhausted its cycle, swaps it with another element based on parity, and generates a new permutation. This continues until all counters reach their maximum values. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize array a with input elements and array c with zeros (counters). 2. Output the initial permutation. 3. Set i = 1 (starting from second position). 4. While i < n: a. If c[i] < i: i. If i is even: swap a[0] and a[i]. ii. If i is odd: swap a[c[i]] and a[i]. iii. Output the new permutation. iv. Increment c[i] by 1. v. Reset i to 1. b. Else: i. Set c[i] = 0. ii. Increment i by 1. Time complexity is O(n*n!) since we generate n! permutations and each takes O(n) time for output. Space complexity is O(n) for arrays a and c. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete counter management system. Common mistakes include incorrect parity handling (even vs odd indices) and forgetting to reset counters properly. Real code pattern: def heaps_permutations(arr): n = len(arr); a = arr[:]; c = [0]*n; result = [a[:]]; i = 1; while i < n: if c[i] < i: if i%2 == 0: a[0], a[i] = a[i], a[0]; else: a[c[i]], a[i] = a[i], a[c[i]]; result.append(a[:]); c[i] += 1; i = 1; else: c[i] = 0; i += 1; return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counter Management in Heap's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the counter array system that tracks processing states for each position.
ladders.explanation: The counter management system is the engine of Heap's algorithm, controlling when to swap elements and generate new permutations. The intuition is that each position i (from 1 to n-1) has a counter c[i] that cycles from 0 to i-1, representing how many times that position has been 'active' in generating permutations for the current configuration. When c[i] < i, we perform a swap and increment c[i]; when c[i] reaches i, we reset it to 0 and move to the next position. This ensures we systematically explore all permutations without repetition. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize c as array of zeros with length n. 2. Set i = 1. 3. While i < n: a. If c[i] < i: process position i (swap based on parity), increment c[i], reset i to 1. b. Else: set c[i] = 0, increment i. This creates a depth-first-like traversal of the permutation tree. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n!) in worst case as it manages counters across all permutations. Space is O(n). It connects to L0 by providing the control flow for the full algorithm. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in counter bounds and incorrect reset logic. Real code pattern: def manage_counters(n): c = [0]*n; i = 1; while i < n: if c[i] < i: # Do swap operation here; c[i] += 1; i = 1; else: c[i] = 0; i += 1; return all permutations generated.

testCases.expected: c cycles through values as in example

testCases.input: Number of counter increments for n=2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Parity-Based Swapping in Heap's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the swap logic that depends on whether current index is even or odd.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of Heap's algorithm is that swaps follow a simple parity rule: if the current index i is even, swap the first element (position 0) with element at i; if i is odd, swap the element at position c[i] (where c is the counter array) with element at i. The intuition is that this alternating pattern ensures all permutations are generated exactly once by systematically varying which elements participate in swaps. For even i, we always involve the first element, creating a rotation effect. For odd i, we use the counter value to select which element to swap with, creating more complex variations. This parity approach is more efficient than recursive backtracking because it uses constant-time swaps and minimal memory. Step-by-step: Given array a, index i, and counter array c: 1. Check if i is even or odd. 2. If even: swap a[0] and a[i]. 3. If odd: swap a[c[i]] and a[i]. Time complexity is O(1) per swap. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the action performed when counters allow it. Common mistakes include swapping wrong indices (e.g., using i instead of c[i] for odd case) or misunderstanding parity (0-based vs 1-based indexing). Real code pattern: def parity_swap(a, i, c): if i % 2 == 0: a[0], a[i] = a[i], a[0]; else: a[c[i]], a[i] = a[i], a[c[i]]; return a.

examples.trace: i=2 is even→swap a[0] and a[2]: a[0]=1, a[2]=3→swap→a becomes [3,2,1].

i=1 is odd→swap a[c[1]] and a[1]: c[1]=1, a[1]=2, a[1]=2→swap a[1] and a[1]? Wait, correct: swap a[c[i]]=a[1] and a[i]=a[1]→actually swap a[1] with itself→no change? Let's re-example: a=[1,2,3], i=1, c=[0,0,0]. i odd→swap a[c[1]]=a[0] and a[1]=a[1]→swap 1 and 2→[2,1,3].

testCases.expected: [1] (no swap as i=0 even but single element)

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Indices with Conditions
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that traverses indices and performs actions based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a loop that goes through array indices from 1 to n-1, checking a condition at each step, and either performing an action or moving to the next index. The intuition is that many algorithms, including Heap's, require controlled iteration where we may reset the loop index based on certain conditions. In Heap's algorithm, we need to repeatedly check if c[i] < i; if true, we do a swap and reset i to 1; if false, we reset c[i] and increment i. This creates a non-linear traversal that efficiently explores all states. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize i = 1. 2. While i < n: a. Check condition (e.g., c[i] < i). b. If true: perform action, modify i (often reset to 1). c. If false: perform cleanup (e.g., reset c[i]), increment i. This pattern allows deep exploration before breadth, similar to depth-first search. Time complexity depends on the condition and actions; here it's part of O(n!). Space is O(1) for loop variables. It connects to L2 by providing the framework where parity swaps occur. Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect reset logic or off-by-one errors in loop bounds. Real code pattern: def iterate_with_condition(n, c): i = 1; while i < n: if c[i] < i: # Action here; i = 1; else: c[i] = 0; i += 1; return.

examples.trace: Start i=1. c[1]=0<1→action, set i=1. i=1: c[1]=1 not <1→c[1]=0, i=2→done.

testCases.expected: i stays 1 (loop not entered as i<1 false)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function to swap two elements at given indices in an array.
ladders.explanation: Swapping is a fundamental operation where two elements in an array exchange positions. The intuition is simple: we need to temporarily store one value, overwrite it with the other, then place the stored value in the other position. This is essential in permutation generation because rearranging elements creates new permutations. Step-by-step: Given array a and indices i and j: 1. Check if i and j are valid indices within array bounds. 2. Store a[i] in a temporary variable temp. 3. Set a[i] = a[j]. 4. Set a[j] = temp. Alternatively, in languages like Python, use tuple assignment: a[i], a[j] = a[j], a[i]. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variable. This connects to L3 as the action performed in the loop when conditions are met. Common mistakes include swapping with out-of-bounds indices or forgetting to handle cases where i == j (swap does nothing). Real code pattern: def swap(a, i, j): if 0 <= i < len(a) and 0 <= j < len(a): temp = a[i]; a[i] = a[j]; a[j] = temp; return a.

examples.trace: i=j=1, swap does nothing: temp = a[1]='b'; a[1]='b'; a[1]='b'; array unchanged.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Understand how to read and write values at specific positions in an array.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices. The intuition is that each position in an array has an index (usually starting at 0), and we can retrieve or change the value at that index directly. This is crucial for algorithms like Heap's because we need to manipulate specific elements during swaps. Key concepts: 1. Indexing: Use a[index] to access element at position index. 2. Assignment: Use a[index] = value to modify element. 3. Bounds: Indices must be within 0 to length-1. 4. Length: len(a) gives number of elements. For example, if a = [10, 20, 30], then a[0] is 10, a[1] is 20, a[2] is 30. Setting a[1] = 25 changes array to [10, 25, 30]. Time complexity for access/assignment is O(1). Space is O(n) for the array. This connects to L4 as the basic operation used in swapping. Common mistakes include using indices outside array bounds or confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing. Real code pattern: def access_and_modify(): a = [1, 2, 3]; val = a[0]; # read; a[1] = 4; # write; return a.

examples.input: Array [5, 10, 15], read index 1, write index 2 with value 20
examples.trace: Initial array: [5,10,15]. Read a[1]: value is 10. Write a[2] = 20: change third element from 15 to 20. New array: [5,10,20].

examples.input: Array ['a','b','c'], read index 0, write index 1 with value 'd'
examples.trace: Initial: ['a','b','c']. Read a[0]: 'a'. Write a[1] = 'd': change second element from 'b' to 'd'. New: ['a','d','c'].
